one_line_takeaway: Ghost defends language-model fine-tuning against gradient inversion by pre-substituting each token with a semantically-distinct but embedding-close 'shadow' token, cutting even state-of-the-art DAGER recovery to under ~8% ROUGE-1 while keeping classification F1 up to 0.92 and perplexity as low as 5.45 - a token-level rather than gradient-level defense.
key_finding: Ghost drives GIA recovery to near-zero (R-1 0.01-0.08, METEOR 0.01-0.06 across all attacks/models) while preserving utility, whereas the strongest attack DAGER achieves near-perfect recovery (R-1 up to 1.00) against no-defense AND against gradient pruning. Gradient noise/pruning only stop early attacks (DLG, TAG) but fail against advanced GRAB/DAGER. Ghost defends up to 98% more tokens than the best baseline. Utility under Ghost stays close to undefended fine-tuning: classification F1 up to 0.92 (accuracy 0.45-0.92) and generation perplexity as low as 5.45, versus the utility-destroying trade-offs of aggressive noise/pruning. Adaptive attacks with full defense knowledge raise recovery only modestly (R-1 0.00-0.29, METEOR 0.00-0.25), so Ghost remains robust.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Ghost (Gradient sHield with Obfuscated tokenS), a defense against gradient inversion attacks (GIAs) in collaborative/federated fine-tuning of language models. Unlike prior gradient-level defenses (noise injection, gradient pruning), Ghost operates at the TOKEN level: before fine-tuning, it replaces each original token with a 'shadow' token that is semantically distinct but close in embedding space. The key insight is that the large token vocabulary contains embedding-proximate yet semantically-unrelated tokens, so substituting them decouples the token space from the embedding/gradient spaces while preserving fine-tuning utility. Ghost has two stages: (1) a SEARCHING step that filters each token's top-k embedding neighbors through three similarity heuristics (indirect similarity via neighbor-set overlap, direct/mutual-neighbor similarity, and common-lemma similarity) to keep only semantically-distinct candidates; and (2) a SELECTION step that uses beam search to pick shadow tokens minimizing the MSE between the hidden states of obfuscated vs. original sequences across all layers. The authors also give a formal analysis (Theorems 1 and 2) arguing the loss difference between original and obfuscated data grows as O(epsilon) while the gradient difference grows as O(1), so utility is preserved while gradient-based reconstruction is disrupted.
threat_model: FedSGD collaborative-learning setting with gradients shared after each local iteration. An honest-but-curious adversary either controls the central server or eavesdrops on client-server communication, and has full knowledge of the language-model architecture and parameters. Its goal is to reconstruct the victim client's private training text from the shared gradient updates. The threat model matches existing GIA papers (one server, one victim client, batch size 1 as the hardest case) and does not exclude auxiliary knowledge such as ground-truth labels or exact sequence lengths, so as not to give the defender an unfair advantage. The paper additionally considers a stronger ADAPTIVE adversary that fully recovers the obfuscated data and has complete knowledge of Ghost's mechanism and hyperparameters (top-k, beam count, overlap ratio), then tries to reverse-engineer the original token from the shadow token.
limitations: (1) Obfuscation happens per-token before training and requires computing hidden states / beam search over shadow candidates for every token, a preprocessing cost the paper does not quantify in wall-clock terms. (2) Utility is not free: on some configurations obfuscated fine-tuning noticeably degrades (e.g., generation perplexity rises to 58.65 on Llama-2-13b Open Australian Legal Corpus vs 3.30 original; classification F1 drops to 0.43 on DeBERTa-v3-base Yahoo). (3) Several attack/defense/model combinations are unsupported due to incompatibility and are simply omitted (DAGER only run on a subset of models). (4) DAGER could not be evaluated against the gradient-noise baseline due to an implementation error, so that utility cell is blank. (5) The theoretical guarantees rest on strong smoothness/small-update assumptions (Assumptions 1-4) and an asymptotic epsilon->0 argument rather than concrete privacy bounds; the analysis argues gradients differ 'significantly' but this is not a formal privacy guarantee like differential privacy. (6) Human evaluation and hyperparameter studies are relegated to appendices and use small samples.

datasets_used: Open Australian Legal Corpus (sentence generation, 1,000 samples)
quantitative_results: Ghost obfuscation similarity to original data: ROUGE-1 0.01-0.12, METEOR 0.01-0.08 across all 21 models/datasets (Table 1)
Ghost defense: GIA recovery R-1 0.01-0.08, METEOR 0.01-0.06 across all five attacks; up to 98% more tokens defended than best baseline
DAGER attack recovery (Table 3): 0.87-1.00 R-1 with no defense AND with gradient pruning, but only 0.02-0.08 R-1 under Ghost (e.g., GPT-2/Enron 1.00 -> 0.04; Llama-2-7b/Enron 0.96 -> 0.02)
Baseline defenses stop early attacks (DLG/TAG: R-1 0.00-0.37) but fail on advanced GRAB/DAGER (R-1 0.13-1.00, METEOR up to 0.98)
Classification utility under Ghost: F1 0.48-0.85, accuracy 0.47-0.84; best obfuscated F1 up to 0.92 (DeBERTa-v3-large/SST-2)
Generation utility under Ghost: perplexity 5.45-19.12 (best 5.45 on Llama-3.1-8b/Open Australian Legal Corpus), loss 1.70-2.95
Obfuscated fine-tuning overall: accuracy 0.45-0.92, F1 0.43-0.92; generation loss 1.70-4.07, perplexity 5.45-58.65
Adaptive attacks (5 strategies, full defense knowledge): R-1 0.00-0.29, METEOR 0.00-0.25 - still low

Theory validation (BERT-base/SST-2): loss deviation 0.47 mean vs gradient deviation 40.21 mean between original and obfuscated data (gradient grows much faster, per Theorems 1 & 2 O(epsilon) loss vs O(1) gradient)
Default hyperparameters: top-k=70, beam=1, overlap threshold tau_o=0.1; baselines set to noise level 0.05 and prune ratio 0.99
baselines_compared: Attacks: DLG, TAG, LAMP (L1+L2 and COS variants), GRAB, DAGER
Defense baselines: gradient noise (DP-SGD-style, Gaussian/Laplacian, level 0.05)
Defense baselines: gradient pruning (LAMP variant, prune ratio 0.99)
Ablation baselines for selection step: random selection, no selection
Configuration baselines: pre-trained model, model fine-tuned on original (undefended) data
